name: Pomelo & Davao Honey Salad
description: A refreshing citrus salad featuring local Davao pomelo drizzled with raw Davao honey and fresh mint.
cookingSteps: Peel and segment the pomelo, removing all white membrane.
Arrange segments in a wide bowl.
Drizzle 1 tablespoon of Davao honey over the pomelo.
Squeeze half a lime over the fruit.
Sprinkle chia seeds and garnish with fresh mint leaves.
Chill for 10 minutes before serving.
source: NutriMind prototype — academic use only (CC BY-NC 4.0 reference: datahiveai/recipes-with-nutrition)

name: Malunggay Egg Drop Soup
description: A classic Filipino soup made with nutrient-dense malunggay leaves and silky egg ribbons in a light broth.
cookingSteps: Sauté minced garlic and onion in a pot with a little oil until fragrant.
Pour in 2 cups of chicken broth and bring to a gentle boil.
Season with 1 teaspoon of fish sauce.
Beat 2 eggs and slowly drizzle into the boiling soup while stirring.
Add a generous handful of malunggay leaves.
Cook for 1 minute more, taste for seasoning, and serve hot.
source: NutriMind prototype — academic use only (CC BY-NC 4.0 reference: datahiveai/recipes-with-nutrition)

name: Bangus Sinigang
description: A tangy tamarind-based soup with milkfish (bangus) and fresh vegetables — a Filipino comfort classic.
cookingSteps: Clean and cut bangus into serving pieces; season with salt.
In a pot, bring 4 cups of water to a boil with tomatoes and onion.
Add tamarind powder and stir until dissolved.
Add radish and string beans; cook for 5 minutes.
Add bangus pieces and simmer for 8 minutes until cooked through.
Add water spinach (kangkong) and cook for 1 minute.
Adjust sourness and salt to taste. Serve with steamed rice.
source: NutriMind prototype — academic use only (CC BY-NC 4.0 reference: datahiveai/recipes-with-nutrition)

name: Grilled Tuna Steak with Toyo-Mansi
description: Fresh yellowfin tuna grilled to perfection with a Davao-style toyo-calamansi marinade.
cookingSteps: Combine soy sauce, calamansi juice, minced garlic, and grated ginger in a bowl.
Marinate tuna steak in the mixture for at least 20 minutes.
Heat a grill pan over high heat and brush with oil.
Grill tuna 3–4 minutes per side for medium doneness.
Season with freshly cracked black pepper.
Serve with steamed vegetables or plain rice.
source: NutriMind prototype — academic use only (CC BY-NC 4.0 reference: datahiveai/recipes-with-nutrition)

name: Tinola Manok
description: Traditional chicken ginger soup with chayote and malunggay — light, nourishing, and deeply comforting.
cookingSteps: Sauté sliced ginger and onion in oil until softened.
Add chicken pieces and brown lightly on all sides.
Pour in 3 cups of rice washing (or plain water) and bring to a boil.
Season with fish sauce; skim any foam.
Add chayote wedges and simmer 10 minutes until tender.
Stir in malunggay leaves and cook 1 more minute.
Serve hot with steamed rice.
source: NutriMind prototype — academic use only (CC BY-NC 4.0 reference: datahiveai/recipes-with-nutrition)

name: Saba Banana Porridge
description: Creamy coconut milk porridge with sliced saba bananas — a warm, budget-friendly Davao breakfast.
cookingSteps: Rinse glutinous rice and combine with 2 cups of water in a pot.
Bring to a boil, then reduce to a simmer, stirring occasionally.
Once rice is soft, pour in half a cup of coconut milk.
Add sliced saba bananas and brown sugar to taste.
Simmer 5 more minutes, stirring gently.
Add a pinch of salt to balance sweetness. Serve warm.
source: NutriMind prototype — academic use only (CC BY-NC 4.0 reference: datahiveai/recipes-with-nutrition)

name: Tuna Omelette
description: A high-protein Filipino-style omelette filled with canned tuna, onions, and tomatoes.
cookingSteps: Drain canned tuna and set aside.
Beat 3 eggs with a pinch of salt and black pepper.
Sauté diced onion and tomato in a pan with oil until softened.
Add tuna and stir for 1 minute.
Pour beaten eggs over the mixture.
Cook on medium heat; fold omelette in half when edges set.
Serve immediately.
source: NutriMind prototype — academic use only (CC BY-NC 4.0 reference: datahiveai/recipes-with-nutrition)

name: Grilled Tilapia with Vegetables
description: Whole tilapia rubbed with spices and grilled, served alongside sautéed local vegetables.
cookingSteps: Score the tilapia and rub with salt, pepper, and minced garlic.
Stuff the cavity with lemon grass.
Grill over medium-high heat for 7 minutes each side until charred.
Meanwhile, sauté sliced squash and string beans in oil with garlic.
Season vegetables with fish sauce and a pinch of sugar.
Plate grilled tilapia alongside the vegetables.
source: NutriMind prototype — academic use only (CC BY-NC 4.0 reference: datahiveai/recipes-with-nutrition)

name: Pinakbet
description: A traditional Ilocano vegetable medley cooked with bagoong (shrimp paste) — nutrient-packed and budget-friendly.
cookingSteps: Sauté garlic, onion, and tomato in oil until softened.
Add bagoong and stir for 1 minute.
Add squash pieces and cook for 3 minutes.
Add eggplant and okra, stir gently.
Add bitter melon last; cook until all vegetables are tender but not mushy.
Adjust saltiness with more bagoong if needed. Serve with rice.
source: NutriMind prototype — academic use only (CC BY-NC 4.0 reference: datahiveai/recipes-with-nutrition)

name: Chicken Adobo
description: The quintessential Filipino braised chicken in vinegar, soy sauce, and bay leaves — perfect for any meal.
cookingSteps: Combine chicken, soy sauce, vinegar, crushed garlic, bay leaves, and peppercorns in a pot.
Marinate for at least 30 minutes (or overnight for deeper flavour).
Bring to a boil over medium-high heat.
Reduce heat and simmer uncovered for 30 minutes, turning chicken occasionally.
When sauce reduces and chicken starts to sizzle, brown slightly.
Serve hot with steamed white rice.
source: NutriMind prototype — academic use only (CC BY-NC 4.0 reference: datahiveai/recipes-with-nutrition)

name: Durian Smoothie Bowl
description: A bold, creamy smoothie bowl made with Davao durian — rich in healthy fats and natural sweetness.
cookingSteps: Blend durian flesh, half a banana, and coconut milk until smooth.
Pour into a wide bowl.
Slice remaining banana and arrange on top.
Sprinkle granola and chia seeds.
Drizzle with Davao honey.
Serve immediately while cold.
source: NutriMind prototype — academic use only (CC BY-NC 4.0 reference: datahiveai/recipes-with-nutrition)

name: Lumpiang Sariwa
description: Fresh spring rolls filled with sautéed vegetables and tofu, wrapped in a thin crepe and drizzled with sweet peanut sauce.
cookingSteps: Sauté garlic, then add tofu cubes and cook until lightly golden.
Add shredded cabbage, julienned carrots, and singkamas.
Season with soy sauce and a pinch of salt; stir-fry until tender.
Allow filling to cool slightly.
Lay lumpia wrapper flat, spoon filling in the centre, and roll tightly.
Drizzle with homemade or store-bought peanut sauce before serving.
source: NutriMind prototype — academic use only (CC BY-NC 4.0 reference: datahiveai/recipes-with-nutrition)

name: Ginataang Mais
description: Sweet coconut milk porridge with young corn kernels — a beloved Filipino afternoon snack.
cookingSteps: Rinse glutinous rice and cook in 1.5 cups water until soft.
Add corn kernels (sliced from cob) and stir.
Pour in coconut milk and mix well.
Add sugar to taste and a pinch of salt.
Simmer on low heat for 5 minutes, stirring constantly.
Serve warm or chilled.
source: NutriMind prototype — academic use only (CC BY-NC 4.0 reference: datahiveai/recipes-with-nutrition)

name: Arroz Caldo
description: Filipino congee with chicken and ginger — warming, filling, and ideal for breakfast or recovery meals.
cookingSteps: Sauté garlic, onion, and ginger in a large pot with oil.
Add chicken pieces and brown on all sides.
Add washed glutinous rice and stir to coat with aromatics.
Pour in 6 cups of water; bring to a boil.
Reduce heat and simmer, stirring occasionally, until rice breaks down (about 30 min).
Season with fish sauce; add more water if too thick.
Serve topped with sliced boiled egg, spring onions, and fried garlic.
source: NutriMind prototype — academic use only (CC BY-NC 4.0 reference: datahiveai/recipes-with-nutrition)

name: Champorado
description: Filipino chocolate rice porridge made with tablea (native cacao) — a hearty, antioxidant-rich breakfast.
cookingSteps: Cook glutinous rice in 3 cups of water over medium heat, stirring often.
Once rice is half-cooked, add crushed tablea discs.
Stir continuously until tablea melts and rice is fully cooked.
Add sugar to taste and a pinch of salt.
Simmer on low until desired consistency is reached.
Serve in bowls drizzled with evaporated milk.
source: NutriMind prototype — academic use only (CC BY-NC 4.0 reference: datahiveai/recipes-with-nutrition)

name: Monggo Soup
description: A classic Filipino mung bean soup with malunggay leaves — packed with plant protein and iron.
cookingSteps: Rinse and soak mung beans for 30 minutes, then drain.
Sauté garlic, onion, and tomato in a pot until softened.
Add mung beans and 4 cups of water; bring to a boil.
Reduce heat and simmer for 30–40 minutes until beans are very soft.
Season with fish sauce.
Add malunggay leaves and cook for 2 more minutes.
Serve hot with steamed rice.
source: NutriMind prototype — academic use only (CC BY-NC 4.0 reference: datahiveai/recipes-with-nutrition)

name: Tortang Talong
description: Filipino eggplant omelette — charred whole eggplant dipped in beaten egg and pan-fried until golden.
cookingSteps: Roast or grill whole eggplants over a flame until skin is charred and flesh is soft.
Peel skin while hot, keeping the stem intact.
Flatten the eggplant flesh gently with a fork.
Beat 2 eggs with salt and pepper in a shallow plate.
Dip eggplant in the egg mixture, coating well.
Pan-fry in hot oil for 3 minutes per side until golden.
Serve with banana ketchup or spiced vinegar.
source: NutriMind prototype — academic use only (CC BY-NC 4.0 reference: datahiveai/recipes-with-nutrition)

name: Paksiw na Bangus
description: Milkfish simmered in vinegar with ginger and vegetables — a tangy, low-fat Filipino staple.
cookingSteps: Clean bangus and cut into serving pieces.
Arrange ginger slices, garlic, and peppercorns at the bottom of a pot.
Layer bangus pieces on top.
Add bitter melon and eggplant slices.
Pour in ½ cup white vinegar and ¼ cup water.
Season with salt; bring to a boil without stirring.
Reduce heat and simmer for 15 minutes. Serve with rice.
source: NutriMind prototype — academic use only (CC BY-NC 4.0 reference: datahiveai/recipes-with-nutrition)

name: Malunggay Fried Rice
description: Day-old rice stir-fried with malunggay, garlic, and egg — a quick, nutrient-dense one-pan meal.
cookingSteps: Heat oil in a wok or large pan over high heat.
Fry minced garlic until golden and fragrant.
Push garlic aside and scramble eggs in the pan.
Add day-old rice and break up any clumps.
Season with soy sauce and a pinch of salt; toss everything together.
Fold in malunggay leaves and stir-fry for 1 more minute.
Serve immediately.
source: NutriMind prototype — academic use only (CC BY-NC 4.0 reference: datahiveai/recipes-with-nutrition)

name: Grilled Liempo with Atchara
description: Juicy pork belly grilled over charcoal, served with tangy papaya atchara — a Davao BBQ favourite.
cookingSteps: Mix soy sauce, calamansi juice, minced garlic, brown sugar, and banana ketchup for the marinade.
Score pork belly and marinate for at least 2 hours or overnight.
Grill over medium-high charcoal heat for 8–10 minutes per side, basting with marinade.
Rest meat for 5 minutes before slicing.
Serve with papaya atchara and steamed rice.
Garnish with calamansi halves on the side.
source: NutriMind prototype — academic use only (CC BY-NC 4.0 reference: datahiveai/recipes-with-nutrition)

name: Utan Bisaya (Visayan Vegetable Soup)
description: A simple but hearty Visayan vegetable soup with squash, string beans, and corn — vegan and budget-friendly.
cookingSteps: Bring 3 cups of water to a boil with onion slices.
Add corn cut into pieces; cook for 5 minutes.
Add squash cubes and string beans.
Simmer for 10 minutes until squash is tender.
Add malunggay leaves and season with salt.
Cook for 1 final minute. Serve as a main dish with rice.
source: NutriMind prototype — academic use only (CC BY-NC 4.0 reference: datahiveai/recipes-with-nutrition)

name: Pork Sisig
description: Sizzling chopped pork with calamansi and chilli — a Filipino bar-food classic, perfect for lunch.
cookingSteps: Boil pork ears and belly until tender; let cool.
Grill or broil until slightly charred for smokiness.
Chop grilled pork finely.
Heat a cast-iron sizzling plate; add a little oil.
Add pork, chopped onion, and chilli; season with soy sauce.
Top with mayonnaise and squeeze calamansi juice over.
Serve sizzling immediately.
source: NutriMind prototype — academic use only (CC BY-NC 4.0 reference: datahiveai/recipes-with-nutrition)
